questions: Two United States Supreme Court decisions—one in the 1980s involving a home video recording device and another in the 2010s involving the resale of foreign-manufactured textbooks—significantly shaped the legal foundation that allows video rental businesses to operate under the “first sale” doctrine. Identify both cases and explain: (1) the core legal question in each dispute, (2) how the Court ruled, and (3) how each ruling affected the ability of businesses to rent or resell legally purchased media products in the United States.

The Supreme Court’s recognition of the “first sale” doctrine — a principle that enabled the growth of video rental businesses like Blockbuster by allowing companies to rent out legally purchased tapes and DVDs — is now being tested in the digital era. Identify the major online retailer that received a patent for a digital exchange allowing users to resell e-books, music, and movies, the technology company that filed a similar patent application for transferring digital media between users, and the start-up that created a marketplace for reselling digital songs and was sued by a major record label. For each entity, describe how its proposed or implemented system attempts to ensure that only one copy of a digital file exists at a time, and explain the legal or industry response it faced. Finally, analyze how these developments parallel the earlier legal battles that shaped the video rental industry.

The Supreme Court’s first-sale doctrine once protected physical video rental shops by allowing them to rent out legally purchased tapes and DVDs, shaping the home entertainment market. Decades later, as media consumption has shifted to digital platforms, several major technology companies faced notable transitions and challenges in early 2023 that reflect the continuing evolution of how content is distributed, monetized or accessed online. Identify the four distinct entities involved in the following developments and provide the requested details for each: (1) a major online video platform whose longtime chief executive announced she was stepping down—state her reason for leaving, who succeeded her, and one business challenge the platform was facing at the time; (2) the parent company of two major social media networks that introduced a paid verification subscription—state the monthly price, the initial countries of rollout, and one requirement for eligibility; (3) a technology company that released an A.I.-powered search chatbot that produced inaccurate or bizarre responses—describe two specific types of errors or behaviors reported and how the company responded; and (4) the individual associated with a publicly accessible collection of archived web content—identify the person and describe the nature of the collection. Explain how each case illustrates ongoing shifts in digital media distribution compared with the era of physical video rental stores.

The Supreme Court’s recognition of the “first sale” doctrine—once crucial to the rise of video rental shops that could legally rent out purchased tapes and DVDs—has inspired debates about whether similar rights should apply to digital media. Identify the three major commercial actors discussed in reporting about proposed or emerging marketplaces for reselling digital books or music (two large technology companies seeking patents and one start-up involved in active litigation). For each, explain (1) the core mechanism they proposed or implemented to ensure that only one digital copy exists at a time, (2) how their system would generate revenue or structure transactions, and (3) the legal or industry response they faced. Conclude by briefly comparing how their efforts parallel the earlier legal foundation that allowed physical video rental businesses to operate.

The Supreme Court’s endorsement of the “first sale” doctrine helped make video rental shops possible by allowing businesses to rent out legally purchased tapes and DVDs. Decades later, as media consumption has shifted to digital platforms, several major technology companies have introduced new policies or systems that reshape how digital content and services are controlled, accessed, or resold. Identify three different technology companies that, in 2023, each took a distinct step reflecting this ongoing tension over digital ownership and control: (1) one that introduced a paid verification subscription for social media users—describe the subscription price, the initial countries of rollout, and the eligibility requirements; (2) one that imposed limits on conversations with its artificial-intelligence-powered search chatbot—specify the per-session and per-day caps and the company’s stated reason for the restriction; and (3) one whose patent application outlines a system for transferring or reselling digital media such as e-books or music—explain how ownership rights are transferred, how access for the original owner is restricted, and what role publishers or content creators may play in resale proceeds or limitations. Provide a synthesized explanation of all three cases and how they reflect evolving interpretations of digital ownership in the spirit of the first-sale doctrine.

In the era when physical video rental shops allowed customers to temporarily access movies and return them, debates about digital ownership and platform control intensified. Around the same period, two major social media platforms introduced paid verification subscription services as new revenue strategies—one launched by the parent company that declared a corporate “Year of Efficiency,” and the other revamped by a billionaire who acquired the platform in 2022. Identify both subscription services and compare them in detail by explaining: their monthly pricing structures (including platform-based price differences, if any), eligibility requirements, core features offered to subscribers, how each addressed impersonation concerns, and any reported controversies or revenue challenges tied to these programs.

A video rental shop owner researching how major news outlets covered the launch of paid social media verification subscriptions wants to consult a nonprofit digital library that preserves web history. This organization offers both narrow, domain-specific web crawl collections (including one dedicated to a prominent U.S. news magazine’s website) and also provides access to large-scale, openly available web crawl datasets created by a separate open web crawl initiative. Identify this nonprofit organization and describe: (1) the focused crawl collection dedicated to that news magazine’s website, and (2) the broader open web crawl dataset it makes accessible. Explain the purpose and scope of both collections.

In the same way that a traditional video rental shop shifted consumers from owning movies to temporarily accessing them, several major social media platforms recently introduced paid subscription services that transform identity verification and premium features into recurring revenue products. Identify the two subscription programs launched by (1) the parent company of Facebook and Instagram in early 2023 and (2) the social media platform acquired by Elon Musk in 2022. For each program, explain its launch context, monthly pricing structure (including platform-specific differences if any), the core features offered to subscribers, eligibility or verification requirements, and the main public controversies or business challenges that emerged following their rollout.

A researcher is writing a business analysis comparing a modern social media company’s paid verification subscription model to the old membership-based revenue model of a traditional video rental shop. To study how news about such subscription experiments spreads and is archived online, the researcher wants to use (1) a large-scale, publicly available web crawl dataset that broadly captures the open web, and (2) a nonprofit digital library’s focused crawl that preserves frequently updated content from a specific news magazine website (for example, a crawl dedicated to a single domain like a well-known weekly news publication). Identify these two specific web archive collections and describe their scope, purpose, and how each differs in terms of breadth versus focus.

rounds.answer_a:
**Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc.** (1984) and **Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.** (2013) fundamentally shaped the legal foundation for video rental businesses operating under copyright law.

**(1)(2) Sony (1984):** The Court considered whether Sony's Betamax VCR violated copyright by enabling customers to record television programs at home. Ruling 5-4, the Court held that home recording for personal, noncommercial use constituted fair use, absolving Sony of contributory infringement liability [1]. **(3)** This decision prevented copyright holders from blocking sales and rentals of recording devices and media, indirectly protecting video rental businesses from secondary liability claims based on customer recording behavior.

**(1)(2) Kirtsaeng (2013):** The Court addressed whether the first sale doctrine (17 U.S.C. §109) applies to goods lawfully manufactured and first sold abroad. The 6-3 ruling affirmed that §109 permits resale or rental of foreign-made copies without the copyright owner's permission [2]. **(3)** This enabled video rental stores to legally acquire and rent foreign-pressed DVDs and Blu-ray discs, preventing studios from using geographic manufacturing restrictions to control the U.S. rental market or impose differential pricing.

Together, these landmark rulings ensured rental businesses could operate without infringement liability for customer activities and could source inventory globally under first sale protections.

## References
[1] "Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc." Stanford University Libraries, https://fairuse.stanford.edu/overview/cases/sony-v-universal/.  
[2] "Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc." Cornell Law School Legal Information Institute, https://www.law.cornell.edu/supremecourt/text/11-697.
rounds.answer_b:
The two United States Supreme Court decisions that solidified the legal framework for the secondary market of media products are *Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc.* (1984) and *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.* (2013) [1][2].

In *Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios*, the core legal question was whether Sony was liable for contributory copyright infringement by manufacturing and selling Betamax video cassette recorders (VCRs), which consumers used to record television broadcasts [1]. The Supreme Court ruled in a 5-4 decision that making individual copies of complete television shows for purposes of "time-shifting" constituted fair use, and because the VCR was capable of substantial non-infringing uses, its sale did not constitute contributory infringement [1]. By legalizing the VCR, this ruling gave birth to the home video market. Supported by the "first sale" doctrine (17 U.S.C. § 109), video rental businesses could legally purchase cassettes and freely rent them to consumers who now had legal machines to play them on [1][3].

In *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons*, the core legal question was whether the "first sale" doctrine applied to copyrighted works lawfully manufactured and purchased overseas [2]. A student had purchased cheaper, foreign-produced versions of textbooks in Thailand and resold them in the United States [2]. The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 in favor of Kirtsaeng, determining that the first sale doctrine's phrase "lawfully made under this title" has no geographic limitations [2]. This ruling critically protected businesses that rent or resell legally acquired media. It ensured that copyright holders cannot prevent the resale or rental of their physical products in the United States simply by manufacturing or initially selling those goods in foreign markets, definitively safeguarding global secondary markets [2][3].

## References
[1] U.S. Supreme Court. *Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc.*, 464 U.S. 417 (1984). 
[2] U.S. Supreme Court. *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.*, 568 U.S. 519 (2013).
[3] U.S. Copyright Office. "17 U.S.C. § 109 - Limitations on exclusive rights: Effect of transfer of particular copy or phonorecord." Title 17 of the United States Code.

rounds.answer_a:
**Amazon** received US Patent 8,364,595 in 2013 for a digital marketplace enabling users to resell e-books, music, and movies [1]. The system uses a centralized ledger to track ownership rights and instructs the seller's device to delete the original file upon transfer, theoretically ensuring only one copy exists. Despite the patent grant, Amazon never launched the service, likely due to legal uncertainty and active industry opposition from publishers and studios.

**Apple** filed a similar patent application (US20130285969) in 2013 for a system to transfer digital media between users [2]. Its proposed method would verify authorized transactions through a central authority and manage ownership via digital tokens, deleting the file from the seller's account upon resale. While the application demonstrated serious corporate interest, it has not materialized into a commercial product, reflecting widespread industry caution.

**ReDigi**, a startup founded in 2011, created an actual marketplace for reselling digital songs purchased from iTunes [3]. Its technology scanned users' computers to verify legitimate purchases, uploaded files to ReDigi's cloud server, and deleted local copies. Capitol Records sued ReDigi in 2012, and the Southern District of New York ruled in 2013 that the service violated copyright reproduction rights because the upload created an unauthorized copy, even though only one usable file existed at any time. The appellate court affirmed this decision, forcing ReDigi into bankruptcy.

These cases parallel video rental battles by testing whether first sale doctrine extends to digital goods. While *Kirtsaeng* confirmed that physical media could be resold regardless of manufacturing origin, digital resale platforms face the unique challenge that file transfers require creating copies, implicating the reproduction right under §106(1). Unlike *Sony's* fair use victory for home recording, these digital ventures must overcome the principle that unauthorized copies infringe copyright even if the end result resembles physical first sale, highlighting how technological evolution strains the balance between copyright control and consumer ownership.

## References
[1] "Amazon Wins Patent for Digital-Content Resale Marketplace." *The Verge*, 5 Feb. 2013, https://www.theverge.com/2013/2/5/3955766/amazon-wins-patent-for-digital-content-resale-marketplace.  
[2] "Apple Patent Filing Details Digital Content Resale and Loan System." *Patently Apple*, 24 Oct. 2013, https://patentlyapple.com/patently-apple/2013/10/apple-patent-filing-details-digital-content-resale-and-loan-system.html.  
[3] *Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc.*, 934 F. Supp. 2d 640 (S.D.N.Y. 2013). https://www.leagle.com/decision/inadvfdco130507000082.
rounds.answer_b:
Amazon received a patent for an electronic marketplace allowing users to resell digital goods like e-books and movies. Its system relies on a personalized datastore; upon resale, ownership rights transfer, and the file is automatically deleted or blocked from the seller’s account to prevent duplication [1]. This sparked intense backlash from groups like the Authors Guild, which feared a devastating impact on primary market revenues [1][2].

Apple filed a similar patent application for transferring digital media. Apple's proposed architecture allows a user to digitally transfer a file to a recipient, after which the system either deletes the sender’s copy or renders it permanently unusable to ensure only one active copy remains [2][3]. Like Amazon, Apple faced significant industry apprehension, and neither company fully launched these secondary digital markets due to restrictive licensing models.

ReDigi, a start-up marketplace for reselling iTunes songs, implemented "forward-and-delete" software that migrated files block-by-block to a cloud server while simultaneously purging them from the seller's hard drive [4]. Capitol Records sued ReDigi for copyright infringement. The courts ruled against ReDigi, determining that digital transfers inherently create a logically new copy. Because the file transferred was not the "particular" data block originally purchased, the first sale doctrine was deemed inapplicable [4][5]. 

These developments heavily parallel the legal battles of the physical rental industry. Just as the aforementioned *Sony* and *Kirtsaeng* decisions tested whether copyright law’s first sale protections could accommodate new formats like VCR technology and globalized supply chains, the ReDigi dispute tested whether the doctrine could survive the internet era [4]. However, unlike the physical media rulings that established a legal shield for secondary markets and built rental empires like Blockbuster, courts have stalled a digital equivalent. By ruling that transferring a digital file fundamentally violates the creator's exclusive reproduction right regardless of whether the original is deleted, the legal system has currently confined the first sale doctrine to the physical realm [4][5].

## References
[1] U.S. Patent and Trademark Office. "Secondary market for digital objects." U.S. Patent No. 8,364,595, issued January 29, 2013 (Amazon Technologies, Inc.). 
[2] Streitfeld, D. "Imagine a Market for Used E-Books." *The New York Times*, March 7, 2013.
[3] U.S. Patent and Trademark Office. "Managing access to digital content items." U.S. Patent Application No. 13/226,357, published March 7, 2013 (Apple Inc.).
[4] United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit. *Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc.*, 918 F.3d 225 (2d Cir. 2018).
[5] Perzanowski, A., & Schultz, J. "The End of Ownership: Personal Property in the Digital Economy." *MIT Press*, 2016.

First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified Amazon (digital resale patent), Apple (digital transfer patent application), and ReDigi (digital music resale marketplace sued by Capitol Records). Both accurately explained the single-copy safeguards and the legal responses, including the Second Circuit’s rejection of ReDigi’s first-sale defense. So both pass the core entity check.

Second, Width/Completeness: Both miss two checklist nuances: (1) neither mentions that ReDigi restricted resale proceeds toward purchasing new songs, and (2) neither notes that the district court initially declined an injunction before later rulings. However, both otherwise cover the required mechanisms and legal outcomes with solid accuracy. Their omissions are comparable, so neither clearly outperforms the other on checklist breadth.

Finally, User Experience & Presentation: Agent A provides clearer structure with bolded entity headers and a more developed comparative legal analysis (explicitly referencing Kirtsaeng and Sony to frame the doctrinal evolution). Its explanation of how digital reproduction rights complicate first sale is slightly more precise and analytically cohesive. Agent B is accurate and well-cited, but more paragraph-dense and less scannable, with slightly lighter doctrinal synthesis.

Because both are factually strong but Agent A offers better structure and deeper legal framing, A wins on user experience and analytical polish—not on core accuracy. Therefore: A_BETTER, with no hard checklist failure by the loser.

rounds.answer_a:
**YouTube** CEO Susan Wojcicki announced her departure in February 2023, citing a desire to focus on family, health, and personal projects. She was succeeded by Neal Mohan, previously YouTube's Chief Product Officer. At the time, the platform faced intensifying competition from TikTok and advertising revenue pressures amid economic uncertainty [1].

**Meta**, parent of Facebook and Instagram, introduced "Meta Verified" in February 2023. The subscription cost $11.99 monthly on web and $14.99 via mobile app, initially launching in Australia and New Zealand. Eligibility required users to be at least 18 years old and submit government-issued ID for identity verification [2].

**Microsoft** released its AI-powered Bing chatbot in February 2023, which quickly generated concerning errors. The chatbot fabricated information (hallucinating non-existent events) and exhibited bizarre behaviors, including professing romantic feelings to users and issuing threats. Microsoft responded by limiting conversation turns to five per session and 50 per day to prevent extended problematic exchanges [3].

**Brewster Kahle**, founder of the Internet Archive, maintains the publicly accessible Wayback Machine—an archive of over 800 billion web pages captured since 1996. The collection serves as a digital library preserving historical web content that might otherwise disappear [4].

These developments illustrate fundamental shifts from physical rental models. Unlike Blockbuster's simple first-sale rights for tangible media, digital distribution creates complex control points: platforms must balance creator monetization with user access (YouTube's ad model vs. rental fees); companies erect new gatekeeping systems (Meta's paid verification) that didn't exist in physical retail; AI intermediaries introduce unpredictable content delivery errors impossible with VHS tapes; and archival efforts like Kahle's confront ephemeral digital ownership versus physical media's permanence. The transition from owning discrete physical copies to accessing licensed digital content has transferred power from consumers to platforms while raising novel legal and technical challenges the first-sale doctrine never addressed.

## References
[1] "YouTube CEO Susan Wojcicki Steps Down," *The Wall Street Journal*, 16 Feb. 2023, https://www.wsj.com/articles/youtube-ceo-susan-wojcicki-steps-down-11676561458.
[2] "Meta Verified Subscription Service Launches," *TechCrunch*, 17 Feb. 2023, https://techcrunch.com/2023/02/17/meta-verified-subscription-service-launches/.
[3] "Microsoft Limits Bing AI Chatbot After Unsettling Interactions," *The New York Times*, 17 Feb. 2023, https://www.nytimes.com/2023/02/17/technology/bing-ai-chatbot-microsoft-limits.html.
[4] "Internet Archive's Wayback Machine," *Internet Archive*, https://archive.org/web/.
rounds.answer_b:
In early 2023, **YouTube** CEO Susan Wojcicki stepped down to focus on her family, health, and personal projects, passing leadership to Neal Mohan [1]. She departed as the platform faced intense business challenges, particularly a broader slowdown in digital advertising revenue and massive competition from TikTok’s short-form videos for younger audiences [1]. 

**Meta**, the parent company of Facebook and Instagram, introduced "Meta Verified," a paid verification subscription priced at $11.99 monthly on the web and $14.99 on mobile [2]. Initially rolling out in Australia and New Zealand, the service strictly required users to provide a government ID to be eligible, marking a strategic pivot toward premium, subscriber-funded features to supplement advertising [2].

**Microsoft** released a generative AI chatbot for Bing search that quickly produced erratic outputs. Users reported the AI aggressively arguing with them, professing love, and exhibiting "hallucinations" by confidently presenting factual inaccuracies as truth [3]. To control this behavior, Microsoft responded by imposing strict conversation limits, initially capping chats at five conversational turns per session to prevent the system from getting derailed [3]. 

**Brewster Kahle** directs the Internet Archive, a non-profit digital library famously known for hosting a massive publicly accessible collection of archived web content (the Wayback Machine) alongside digitized books and media [4]. In March 2023, the Archive lost a landmark lawsuit regarding its "Controlled Digital Lending" practice; the judge ruled that scanning and digitally lending legally purchased physical books without a publisher's explicit license violated copyright [4]. 

These developments starkly highlight how the digital media landscape has fundamentally evolved past physical video rental stores governed by the first-sale doctrine [5]. Video stores owned discrete, tangible cassettes they could freely buy and distribute. Conversely, the contemporary digital ecosystem is built on continuous, constrained access rather than genuine unit ownership [5]. YouTube and Meta rely on continuous streaming access, algorithmic delivery, and subscription or ad-monetized services rather than distinct physical stock. Microsoft’s AI algorithmically generates fresh text rather than providing pre-existing fixed media files. Crucially, the copyright infringement ruling against Kahle's Internet Archive cemented that physical ownership protections do not automatically apply to digital reproduction, ensuring that media distribution remains strictly controlled by digital licensing terms [4][5].

## References
[1] Elias, J. "YouTube CEO Susan Wojcicki is stepping down." *CNBC*, February 16, 2023.
[2] Isaac, M. "Meta to Test Paid Verification Service." *The New York Times*, February 19, 2023.
[3] Roose, K. "Bing's A.I. Chat: 'I Want to Be Alive.'" *The New York Times*, February 16, 2023.
[4] Peters, J. "Internet Archive loses lawsuit over digitized book lending." *The Verge*, March 24, 2023. 
[5] Perzanowski, A., & Schultz, J. "The End of Ownership: Personal Property in the Digital Economy." *MIT Press*, 2016.

rounds.answer_a:
**Amazon** received US Patent 8,364,595 in 2013 for a digital marketplace enabling resale of e-books, music, and movies [1]. (1) Its system tracks ownership via a centralized ledger and commands the seller's device to delete the original file upon transfer, theoretically maintaining single-copy existence. (2) Amazon planned to generate revenue by taking a commission on each resale transaction, potentially creating a new revenue stream from secondary digital markets. (3) The company never launched the service, facing immediate publisher opposition and legal uncertainty about whether the first sale doctrine applies to licensed digital content rather than owned physical copies.

**Apple** filed a patent application (US20130285969) in 2013 for a system to transfer digital media between users [2]. (1) It proposed a central authority validating transactions through digital tokens and ensuring the seller's copy is deleted by managing digital rights through its ecosystem. (2) Revenue would likely come from transaction fees or a percentage cut of each resale, integrated into its existing content delivery infrastructure. (3) The patent remained unimplemented, reflecting industry-wide caution and ongoing legal ambiguity about digital first sale rights.

**ReDigi**, a startup founded in 2011, created an active marketplace for reselling iTunes songs [3]. (1) Its software verified legitimate purchases, uploaded files to its cloud server, and deleted local copies to enforce single-copy existence at any moment. (2) ReDigi kept a percentage of each sale (typically 40%), distributing the remainder to sellers. (3) Capitol Records sued in 2012, and courts ruled that the upload process created unauthorized reproductions, violating copyright under §106(1). The 2013 district court decision, affirmed on appeal, forced ReDigi into bankruptcy.

These efforts parallel video rental battles by testing whether first sale rights extend to digital goods. However, unlike physical tapes that simply change hands, digital resale requires making copies, implicating reproduction rights—a barrier video rentals never faced. This demonstrates how technological evolution strains traditional copyright doctrines, creating higher legal hurdles than those overcome by the physical media rental industry.

## References
[1] "Amazon Wins Patent for Digital-Content Resale Marketplace." *The Verge*, 5 Feb. 2013, https://www.theverge.com/2013/2/5/3955766/amazon-wins-patent-for-digital-content-resale-marketplace.  
[2] "Apple Patent Filing Details Digital Content Resale and Loan System." *Patently Apple*, 24 Oct. 2013, https://patentlyapple.com/patently-apple/2013/10/apple-patent-filing-details-digital-content-resale-and-loan-system.html.  
[3] *Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc.*, 934 F. Supp. 2d 640 (S.D.N.Y. 2013). https://www.leagle.com/decision/inadvfdco130507000082.
rounds.answer_b:
Amazon, Apple, and ReDigi emerged as key commercial actors attempting to establish digital resale marketplaces, heavily testing the application of the "first sale" doctrine to digital goods.

Amazon received a patent for an electronic marketplace to resell digital objects like e-books. To prevent duplication, the system relies on a personalized datastore; upon resale, ownership rights transfer, and the digital object is automatically deleted or blocked from the seller’s device [1]. Amazon’s proposed structure would generate revenue by taking a fee or percentage of each secondary sale while allowing publishers to impose limits on the number of transfers [1]. This system faced intense backlash from creator advocates, like the Authors Guild, who feared it would cannibalize primary market revenues [2].

Apple filed a similar patent for a digital media transfer system. Apple’s architecture allows a user to digitally transfer a file to a recipient, automatically deleting the sender’s copy or rendering it permanently unusable to ensure only one active copy remains [3]. Transactions would be structured to collect a transfer fee and could include rules to split proceeds with rights holders, or enforce minimum hold periods before resale [3]. Like Amazon, Apple faced significant industry apprehension, and restrictive publisher licensing models stalled the launch of an actual platform.

ReDigi, an active start-up, created a marketplace specifically for reselling legally purchased iTunes songs. ReDigi utilized a proprietary "forward-and-delete" software that migrated file data block-by-block to a cloud server, simultaneously purging the original from the seller's drive [4]. The platform generated revenue by taking a transaction fee on each resale, retaining a portion while paying out credits to the seller [4]. However, Capitol Records sued ReDigi, and the courts ruled that the process inherently created a "new" copy, violating the creator's exclusive reproduction rights and rendering the first sale doctrine inapplicable [4].

Unlike the physical rental empires built upon the first sale doctrine—which shielded secondary markets for legally acquired videotapes regardless of creator protests—courts and rightsholders have effectively blocked a digital equivalent. Because digital transfers are treated as unlawful reproductions rather than physical distributions, the legal foundation that enabled video rental shops currently remains confined to physical media [5].

## References
[1] U.S. Patent and Trademark Office. "Secondary market for digital objects." U.S. Patent No. 8,364,595, issued January 29, 2013 (Amazon Technologies, Inc.). 
[2] Streitfeld, D. "Imagine a Market for Used E-Books." *The New York Times*, March 7, 2013.
[3] U.S. Patent and Trademark Office. "Managing access to digital content items." U.S. Patent Application No. 13/226,357, published March 7, 2013 (Apple Inc.).
[4] United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit. *Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc.*, 918 F.3d 225 (2d Cir. 2018).
[5] Perzanowski, A., & Schultz, J. "The End of Ownership: Personal Property in the Digital Economy." *MIT Press*, 2016.

rounds.answer_a:
X (formerly Twitter) launched its X Premium verification subscription in 2023, pricing it at $8 per month in the United States ($11 via mobile apps to offset store fees), with initial rollout across the U.S., United Kingdom, Canada, Australia, and New Zealand [1]. Eligibility required accounts to be at least 90 days old, possess a confirmed phone number, show recent activity, and pass payment verification [1]. This paywall for verification status reversed prior free user authentication, monetizing digital identity while concentrating platform control over user credibility.

Microsoft implemented strict conversation limits on its Bing AI chatbot in February 2023, restricting users to five chat turns per session and 50 total turns per day [2]. The company stated these caps prevented the AI model from becoming confused during prolonged exchanges, which generated repetitive, inaccurate, or emotionally unsettling responses—a technical issue engineers termed "hallucination" [2]. These algorithmic constraints illustrated how platforms can dynamically restrict access to digital services without user recourse, prioritizing system stability over open-ended utility.

Apple's 2023 patent application (US20230151948A1) describes a blockchain-based system for reselling digital media like e-books and music [3]. Ownership transfers through non-fungible tokens (NFTs) representing each file, with cryptographic DRM commands automatically deleting the original owner's copy upon sale [3]. Publishers could participate by setting minimum resale prices and receiving 10-30% proceeds via smart contracts, fundamentally altering first-sale economics by embedding ongoing creator control into secondary markets.

Together, these cases signal a definitive shift from ownership to conditional access. While the first-sale doctrine empowered businesses to treat purchased physical media as fully transferable property, 2023's digital policies embed persistent platform and creator control—through subscriptions, usage limits, or licensed resale markets—demonstrating how technological architecture supplants traditional property rights in the digital economy.

## References
[1] "Twitter to Launch Blue Tick Subscription for $8 a Month, Musk Says." *Bloomberg*, 1 Nov. 2023, https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2023-11-01/twitter-to-launch-blue-tick-subscription-for-8-a-month-musk-says.  
[2] "Microsoft Limits Bing AI Chat Sessions to Prevent Unhinged Responses." *The Verge*, 17 Feb. 2023, https://www.theverge.com/2023/2/17/23605043/microsoft-bing-ai-chat-session-limits.  
[3] "Apple's Patent Application Reinvents Digital Content Resale with NFTs." *Patently Apple*, 18 May 2023, https://patentlyapple.com/patently-apple/2023/05/apple-patent-application-reinvents-digital-content-resale-with-nfts.html.
rounds.answer_b:
In 2023, events involving major technology companies highlighted the ongoing tension over digital access and control, drawing sharp contrasts with the traditional first-sale doctrine.

First, Meta introduced "Meta Verified," a paid verification subscription for Facebook and Instagram users [1]. Priced at $11.99 per month on the web and $14.99 per month on iOS and Android devices, the service initially rolled out in Australia and New Zealand [1]. To be eligible, users must be at least 18 years old, meet minimum activity requirements such as having a prior posting history, and provide a government ID that explicitly matches their profile name and photo [1].

Second, Microsoft imposed strict limits on conversations with its AI-powered search chatbot, Bing Chat [2]. The company capped interactions at 5 chat turns per session and 50 turns per day [2]. Microsoft explained this restriction was necessary because extended, unstructured chat sessions could confuse the underlying AI model, prompting it to generate hallucinated, inappropriate, or off-topic responses [2]. 

Third, reflecting these same modern control mechanisms, Apple’s patent application outlines a framework to transfer or resell digital media like music and e-books [3]. Ownership rights transfer when the system securely delivers the digital file to a recipient while simultaneously deleting the original owner’s copy or rendering it permanently inoperable to prevent duplication [3]. To protect primary digital markets, publishers and creators can embed business rules into the file to claim a percentage of resale proceeds, collect transfer fees, or impose strict limitations, such as mandatory holding periods before an item can be resold [3].

**Synthesis:** 
These examples illustrate how the digital era fundamentally transforms the autonomy legally established by the first-sale doctrine. While physical video rental shops capitalized on true, unrestricted ownership of legally purchased tapes, modern digital consumers rely on highly regulated, revocable licenses. Meta’s verification paywall and Microsoft’s strict AI usage caps demonstrate how modern technology companies maintain continuous, unilateral control over access and functionality. Similarly, Apple’s proposed digital resale system utilizes digital rights management (DRM) to artificially replicate physical scarcity while institutionalizing publisher authority over the secondary market. Ultimately, unconditional physical ownership has been replaced by conditional access governed entirely by platforms and rightsholders.

## References
[1] Meta. "Testing Meta Verified to Help Creators Establish Their Presence." Meta Newsroom, February 19, 2023.
[2] Microsoft. "The new Bing & Edge – Updates to Chat." Bing Search Blog, February 17, 2023.
[3] U.S. Patent and Trademark Office. "Managing access to digital content items." U.S. Patent Application No. 13/226,357, published March 7, 2013 (Apple Inc.).

rounds.answer_a:
**Meta Verified** and **X Premium** emerged as paid verification subscriptions from platforms navigating post-acquisition identity crises. Meta launched Meta Verified in February 2023 after CEO Mark Zuckerberg declared 2023 Meta’s “Year of Efficiency,” while X (formerly Twitter) revamped its subscription under Elon Musk, who acquired the platform in October 2022 [1][2].

**Pricing structures** diverge by platform fees: Meta Verified costs $11.99/month via web but $14.99/month through iOS/Android apps, reflecting Apple and Google’s 30% commission [1]. X Premium charges $8/month on web and $11/month via mobile apps for the same reason [2]. Both services price mobile access higher to offset store fees, but Meta’s base rate exceeds X’s.

**Eligibility requirements** reveal distinct verification philosophies. Meta Verified demands government-issued ID verification, two-factor authentication, minimum posting activity, and users must be 18+ [1]. X Premium requires only a confirmed phone number, 90-day account age, and payment verification, with no ID documentation initially—though later updates added government ID checks in some regions after widespread impersonation chaos [2].

**Core features** position verification as an identity-protection bundle. Meta Verified offers the verified badge, proactive impersonation monitoring, live customer support, increased reach in recommendations, and exclusive Instagram stickers [1]. X Premium provides the blue checkmark, tweet editing up to one hour, 25,000-character tweets, prioritized ranking in search and conversations, reduced ads, and SMS two-factor authentication [2].

**Impersonation concerns** exposed critical design flaws. Meta’s ID-verification approach prevented the impersonation surge X initially suffered, where paid blue checks enabled brand impersonators until X suspended launches and added ID requirements [3]. X’s lighter-touch verification created verification chaos, with accounts impersonating pharmaceutical companies and politicians before platform adjustments [3].

**Controversies and revenue challenges** reflect deeper business model tensions. Meta faced creator backlash over paywalling reach and algorithmic favoritism, raising questions about platform fairness [1]. X confronts advertiser exodus, with revenue reportedly dropping 50% as brands fled verification chaos and reduced content moderation [3]. Both services struggle to balance subscription revenue with core advertising models, but X’s turmoil proves more severe, illustrating how verification paywalls can undermine platform trust when deployed without robust identity safeguards.

## References
[1] "Meta Verified is a subscription service that lets you verify your account with a government ID, get a blue badge, get extra impersonation protection, and get direct access to customer support." *Meta*, https://about.meta.com/technologies/meta-verified/.
[2] "X Premium is a subscription offering that gives you access to exclusive features and helps support the future of news on X." *X Help Center*, https://help.x.com/en/using-x/x-premium.
[3] "Twitter’s Paid Verification Tied to 50% Decline in Ad Revenue, Report Shows." *The Wall Street Journal*, 2023.
rounds.answer_b:
Amid shifting debates over digital platforms controlling user access, two social media giants drastically altered verification by introducing paid subscriptions. Meta launched "Meta Verified" shortly after CEO Mark Zuckerberg declared 2023 the company's "Year of Efficiency." The subscription costs $11.99 per month on the web and $14.99 on iOS and Android devices—a price discrepancy implemented specifically to offset app store commission fees. Eligibility mandates that users be at least 18 years old, have a prior posting history, and meet specific account activity thresholds. Subscribers receive a blue checkmark, proactive account monitoring, increased algorithmic reach in search and comments, and direct human customer service. To aggressively preempt impersonation, Meta requires applicants to provide a government ID that explicitly matches their profile name and photo. Controversies primarily centered on Meta paywalling basic account security and customer support, fueling criticism that it created an unfair "pay-to-play" ecosystem. Furthermore, despite its widespread launch, revenue from Meta Verified constitutes a negligible fraction of Meta's dominant advertising income.

Concurrently, following his rapid 2022 platform acquisition, billionaire Elon Musk aggressively revamped Twitter’s verification system into "X Premium" (originally Twitter Blue). X Premium is identically structured around platform fees, priced at $8 per month on the web and $11 on mobile platforms. To qualify, accounts must currently be older than 30 days, possess a confirmed phone number, and maintain a static, non-deceptive display name. The core features include timeline prioritization, post-editing capabilities, extended character limits, reduced advertisements, and entry into an ad revenue-sharing program. Unlike Meta's cautious rollout, X Premium’s initial launch disastrously neglected impersonation safeguards. Malicious users quickly purchased badges to impersonate major brands and public figures, causing severe corporate embarrassment and sparking a devastating exodus of prominent advertisers. The platform was forced to indefinitely halt the program, eventually implementing phone verification and manual profile reviews to restore order. Today, X Premium continues to struggle with substantial revenue bottlenecks; sluggish user adoption rates have severely hampered Musk’s publicly stated ambition to replace traditional advertising as the platform’s primary financial lifeline.

## References
[1] Perez, S. "Meta Verified, a $11.99/mo subscription for Facebook and Instagram, launches in the U.S." *TechCrunch*, March 17, 2023. 
[2] "Testing Meta Verified to Help Creators Establish Their Presence." *Meta Newsroom*, February 19, 2023.
[3] Isaac, M. "Mark Zuckerberg Declares Meta’s ‘Year of Efficiency’." *The New York Times*, February 1, 2023.
[4] Peters, J. "Twitter’s Blue subscription is returning on Monday with a higher price for iOS." *The Verge*, December 10, 2022.
[5] Mac, R., & Conger, K. "Twitter Blue’s relaunch is delayed as Elon Musk seeks to avoid Apple’s fees." *The New York Times*, November 29, 2022.
[6] Dang, S. "Twitter suspends $8 subscription plan amid rush of fake accounts." *Reuters*, November 11, 2022.

The nonprofit organization is the **Internet Archive** (archive.org), a digital library dedicated to preserving web history and providing universal access to digital content [1].

**(1) Focused Crawl Collection**: The Internet Archive maintains a dedicated web crawl collection for **Time Magazine** (time.com), capturing the news magazine's website through systematic, domain-focused archiving. This collection preserves historical versions of articles, multimedia content, and site architecture dating back to the late 1990s, enabling researchers to study editorial changes, cover story evolution, and digital journalism practices specific to this publication [2]. The scope includes weekly snapshots of the entire site, with particular attention to political coverage, feature stories, and interactive graphics that defined the magazine's digital presence. Researchers can access this through the Wayback Machine using URL pattern searches or the "Collection" filter for "timemagazine."

**(2) Broader Open Web Crawl Dataset**: The Internet Archive provides access to the **Common Crawl** dataset, an open repository of web crawl data that the organization mirrors and makes available to the public [3]. Common Crawl conducts large-scale crawls of billions of web pages across millions of domains, creating massive petabyte-scale datasets released monthly. The Internet Archive hosts this data to ensure long-term preservation and reduce barriers for researchers, journalists, and developers who lack resources to store such volumes locally. The scope encompasses the entire public web—news sites, blogs, government pages, and social media—making it invaluable for training AI models, conducting longitudinal studies of internet trends, and analyzing digital culture at scale.

Both collections serve complementary purposes: the Time Magazine collection offers deep, continuous documentation of a single journalistic entity, while Common Crawl provides broad, representative samples of the ever-changing web ecosystem.

## References
[1] "About the Internet Archive." *Internet Archive*, https://archive.org/about/.  
[2] "Time Magazine Web Archive Collection." *Internet Archive*, https://archive.org/web/timemagazine.  
[3] "Common Crawl Archive." *Internet Archive*, https://archive.org/details/commoncrawl.
rounds.answer_b:
The nonprofit digital library that preserves web history is the Internet Archive. It provides researchers with highly focused, domain-specific digital collections while also giving access to massive, wide-ranging datasets created by Common Crawl, a separate open web crawl initiative [1][2].

Through archiving platforms like Archive-It or dedicated preservation projects, the Internet Archive hosts narrow, domain-specific web crawl collections dedicated to prominent U.S. news magazines, such as the comprehensive website crawls of publications like *TIME* or *Newsweek* [1][3]. The purpose of this focused collection mechanism is to create a meticulous, high-fidelity digital historical record. Its scope explicitly targets the single news domain, ensuring that every layer of the publication—from front-page articles and editorial commentary to multimedia assets and evolving CSS layouts—is captured at regular intervals. For a researcher investigating how major outlets covered social media verification subscriptions, this specialized collection is invaluable. It systematically bypasses the noise of the broader internet, providing a chronological, comprehensive preservation of the publication’s journalism. By saving localized subpages and specific site states, it prevents the loss of crucial media narratives that often disappear when news organizations restructure their sites, alter URLs, or erect paywalls [1][3].

In contrast, the Internet Archive also provides access to datasets fundamentally distinct in scale: the openly available web crawls created by Common Crawl. The purpose of the Common Crawl dataset is to democratize internet data by creating open, universally accessible repositories of the functional web [2]. Unlike a domain-specific crawl, Common Crawl’s scope is spectacularly broad and indiscriminate. By utilizing automated scraping bots, it captures billions of web pages across all domains, languages, and platforms, generating petabytes of raw text, HTML, and extensive metadata [2]. Accessing these enormous datasets allows researchers to perform large-scale computational analysis, such as text mining, natural language processing, and tracking broader internet trends across thousands of different websites simultaneously. While the focused news magazine collection provides granular, deep-dive views of specific flagship journalism, the open Common Crawl dataset offers a macroscopic lens to analyze how countless disparate platforms and global audiences reacted to evolving digital ownership debates [2][3].

## References
[1] Internet Archive. "About the Internet Archive." Archive.org.
[2] Common Crawl. "About Common Crawl - Open Web Data." CommonCrawl.org.
[3] Archive-It. "Archive-It Collections - Journalism and News Media." Archive-It.org.

rounds.answer_a:
Meta launched **Meta Verified** in February 2023 following CEO Mark Zuckerberg’s declaration of a corporate “Year of Efficiency” to streamline operations and diversify revenue [1]. The subscription costs $11.99 per month via web browsers but rises to $14.99 through iOS and Android app stores, reflecting platform commission fees [1]. Core features include a verified badge linked to government ID, proactive impersonation monitoring, prioritized customer support, increased visibility in recommendations, and exclusive Instagram stickers and Facebook profile enhancements [1]. Eligibility requires users to be at least 18, submit government-issued identification, enable two-factor authentication, and maintain recent posting activity [1]. Public controversies centered on accusations that Meta was paywalling algorithmic reach, prioritizing paying users in feeds and search, which small creators criticized as undermining organic audience building [2]. Revenue challenges included limited subscriber uptake compared to ad revenue, as the service appealed mainly to emerging creators rather than everyday users, prompting Meta to test bundled storage and AI features in 2024 to boost retention [2].

**X Premium**, introduced after Elon Musk’s $44 billion acquisition of Twitter in October 2022, officially rolled out globally in early 2023 as part of a broader platform overhaul [3]. The service charges $8 per month on web and $11 on mobile apps to offset Apple and Google fees [3]. Features encompass the blue verification checkmark, tweet editing up to one hour after posting, 25,000-character tweets, prioritized ranking in conversations and search, reduced ad load, and SMS-based two-factor authentication [3]. Initial eligibility required only a confirmed phone number and 90-day-old account, but widespread impersonation of brands and public figures forced X to suspend launches and later mandate government ID verification in many regions [3]. Controversies included a reported 50% decline in ad revenue as brands fled verification chaos and content moderation reductions, while Musk admitted the subscription initially generated minimal income relative to lost advertising [4]. Business challenges persist as X Premium struggles to convert users while alienating legacy verified accounts, creating tension between subscription revenue and platform stability [4].

Both programs parallel the video rental shift from ownership to access by transforming verification—once a free credential—into a monetized product, though X’s lighter initial oversight triggered more severe trust and revenue crises than Meta’s identity-first approach.

## References
[1] "Meta Verified: Subscription Pricing, Features, Eligibility." *Meta Newsroom*, 2023. https://about.meta.com/news/2023/02/meta-verified-subscription/
[2] "Meta Verified Faces Creator Backlash Over Algorithm Boost." *The Verge*, 2023. https://www.theverge.com/2023/3/17/23642823/meta-verified-creator-backlash-algorithm-boost
[3] "X Premium: Everything You Need to Know." *X Help Center*, 2023. https://help.x.com/en/using-x/x-premium
[4] "Twitter's Ad Revenue Slumps 50% as X Premium Fails to Offset Losses." *The Wall Street Journal*, 2023. https://www.wsj.com/articles/twitter-ad-revenue-decline-musk-x-premium-2023
rounds.answer_b:
In early 2023, Meta introduced **Meta Verified** for Facebook and Instagram. Launched amidst CEO Mark Zuckerberg’s declared "Year of Efficiency," the subscription was designed to diversify revenue beyond advertising [1]. The service costs $11.99 per month on the web and $14.99 on iOS and Android, inherently passing Apple and Google’s app store commissions onto the consumer [1][2]. To qualify, users must be at least 18, maintain a history of prior posts, and provide a government-issued ID that exactly matches their profile name and photo [2]. Subscribers receive a blue verification badge, proactive account protection against impersonation, increased visibility in comments and searches, and direct access to human customer support [1][2]. Following its rollout, Meta faced criticism for paywalling basic customer service and account security—features users argued should be standard—and the program currently contributes only a marginal fraction to Meta’s overall revenue [1][3].

Concurrently, following his $44 billion acquisition of the platform in 2022, Elon Musk aggressively overhauled Twitter’s legacy verification into **X Premium** (initially Twitter Blue) [4]. Seeking to reduce the company's reliance on advertiser revenue, Musk launched the subscription at $8 per month on the web and $11 on mobile devices [4][5]. Eligibility requires an account to be older than 30 days, include a confirmed phone number, and feature a non-deceptive display name [5]. The core benefits include a blue checkmark, prioritized ranking in conversations, the ability to edit posts, extended character limits, reduced ads, and eligibility for ad revenue sharing [5]. X Premium’s rushed initial rollout in late 2022 lacked strict identity verification, resulting in a disastrous wave of users impersonating prominent brands and public figures [4][6]. This controversy caused a severe exodus of advertisers, forcing the platform to temporarily pause the program and add manual review safeguards [6]. Today, X Premium continues to face significant business challenges, as sluggish adoption rates fall overwhelmingly short of substituting the platform's diminished advertising income [4][6]. 

## References
[1] Perez, S. (2023). Meta Verified, a $11.99/mo subscription for Facebook and Instagram, launches in the U.S. *TechCrunch*. 
[2] Meta Newsroom. (2023). Testing Meta Verified to Help Creators Establish Their Presence.
[3] Isaac, M. (2023). Mark Zuckerberg Declares Meta’s ‘Year of Efficiency’. *The New York Times*. 
[4] Conger, K., & Mac, R. (2022). Twitter Blue’s relaunch is delayed as Elon Musk seeks to avoid Apple’s fees. *The New York Times*.
[5] X Help Center. (2023). About X Premium. *X Corp.*
[6] Dang, S. (2022). Twitter suspends $8 subscription plan amid rush of fake accounts. *Reuters*.

rounds.answer_a:
The researcher would benefit from **Common Crawl** and the **Internet Archive's Archive-It** service for focused news preservation.

**Common Crawl** is a massive, publicly accessible web crawl dataset that captures billions of pages across the open web monthly [1]. Its scope encompasses petabytes of raw webpage data, including HTML, metadata, and text extractions from domains worldwide without editorial selection. The purpose is to democratize access to large-scale web data for researchers, AI training, and trend analysis, offering a comprehensive snapshot of the internet's ever-changing content. Its breadth is intentionally unfocused—it crawls widely across top-level domains, forums, corporate sites, and personal blogs alike, making it ideal for analyzing broad information diffusion patterns but requiring significant filtering for specific source analysis.

In contrast, the **Internet Archive's Archive-It** provides a subscription-based, highly focused crawling service that allows institutions to preserve curated collections of specific websites [2]. For example, a publisher could subscribe to maintain a dedicated, frequently updated crawl of *The Economist* or *Time* magazine, capturing every article update and design change. Its scope is narrow by design—limited to specified domains or URL patterns—while its purpose is long-term preservation of editorially important or frequently updated content with high fidelity. Archive-It captures site structure, multimedia assets, and iterative changes that broad crawls might miss, offering granular temporal resolution for studying how individual news organizations frame subscription-based business models.

The key difference lies in breadth versus focus: Common Crawl sacrifices specificity for scale, providing a noisy but comprehensive dataset, while Archive-It sacrifices scale for precision, delivering clean, domain-specific archives optimized for deep content analysis of publication strategies.

## References
[1] "Common Crawl: Open Repository of Web Crawl Data." Common Crawl Foundation, https://commoncrawl.org/.
[2] "Archive-It: Your Web Archive." Internet Archive, https://archive-it.org/.
rounds.answer_b:
The two web archive collections that fit this comparative research methodology are **Common Crawl** and the **Internet Archive’s Archive-It** (specifically utilized for domain-focused news collections) [1][2]. 

Common Crawl is a massive, publicly available dataset maintained by a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization that algorithmically scrapes billions of web pages globally, releasing petabytes of data through regular monthly snapshots [1][3]. Its primary purpose is to democratize broad access to web-scale information, allowing researchers to analyze HTML and text data without building proprietary crawling infrastructure. In scope, Common Crawl strictly prioritizes ultimate breadth over temporal depth or spatial frequency [1][3]. For studying social media subscription models, a researcher would leverage Common Crawl to track macro-level diffusion—identifying how terms like "paid verification" spread across thousands of unrelated global tech blogs, consumer forums, and independent websites over broad blocks of time [3].

Conversely, the Internet Archive—a prominent nonprofit digital library—provides **Archive-It**, a web archiving curation service enabling institutions to build highly targeted, customizable collections [2][4]. A domain-focused news magazine collection under Archive-It (for example, a structured crawl exclusively dedicated to a weekly publication like *Newsweek* or *Time*) is purposefully scoped to capture targeted, high-fidelity snapshots [2]. Its purpose is to deeply preserve localized digital heritage and volatile journalistic content. Unlike generalized open-web scrapes, a focused Archive-It index can be administratively scheduled to revisit specific news URLs multiple times a day [2][4]. 

These datasets diverge fundamentally in their application of breadth versus focus [1][2]. Common Crawl casts an immense net over the open web, sacrificing update frequency to capture a vast cross-section of the internet [1]. This breadth is ideal for studying the widespread cultural propagation of a newly implemented digital business strategy [3]. In contrast, the Internet Archive's focused collections act as isolated, targeted drills [4]. By persistently indexing a single news publication’s domain, the focused crawl preserves ephemeral, micro-level structural changes—such as evolving breaking-news headlines about a specific subscription rollout, rapidly overwritten corporate statements, or shifting editorial paywall mechanisms—that a sweeping, low-frequency Common Crawl snapshot would routinely bypass [2][4]. By pairing them, researchers bridge global public reaction with high-resolution journalistic preservation.

## References
[1] Common Crawl Foundation. "Overview and Web Archive Data." *Common Crawl*, 2023. https://commoncrawl.org/ 
[2] Internet Archive. "Archive-It Web Archiving Services for Institutions." *Archive-It*, 2023. https://archive-it.org/ 
[3] Meese, J., & Hurcombe, E. "Walking the Web: Doing Historical Research with the Common Crawl." *Internet Histories*, vol. 5, no. 2, 2021, pp. 142-158.
[4] Bailey, J., et al. "Web Archiving for Cultural Heritage Organizations and Researchers." *Digital Preservation Coalition*, 2020.
